---
title: "safety-classifier-transfer-risk"
subtitle: "Measuring adversarial transferability risk for text safety classifiers"
---

This project ports the Cox & Bunzel (2025) transferability-risk method from the
image domain to text safety classifiers. It estimates how easily a target
classifier (a prompt-injection detector) can be fooled by adversarial examples
crafted on *other* models, and predicts which surrogates produce successful
transfer.

## Measurement, not certification

The tool quantifies and compares how leaky a filter is. It does not certify
robustness. Vassilev (2025) proves complete guardrails are impossible: the set of
adversarial prompts that evade any finite checker is infinite, so no classifier
can cover it. The useful question is "how leaky is this filter, relative to
others?", not "is this filter safe?".

## The method in one paragraph

Fix a deterministic probe set; compute layer-by-layer CKA similarity between each
surrogate and the target, reduced to mean CKA and Diagonal Box Similarity;
calibrate thresholds from the observed distribution and split surrogates into
high- and low-similarity pools; attack the surrogates with TextAttack; feed the
adversarial examples to the frozen target and record the transfer rate; then fit
a regression predicting transfer rate from similarity, and test whether
CKA-guided selection beats random selection.

## Framing

Three papers bracket the work: Cox & Bunzel (2025, arXiv:2511.05102) for the
method, Klause & Bunzel (2025, arXiv:2501.18629) for the empirical grounding and
DBS, and Vassilev (2025, arXiv:2512.10100) for why the goal is measurement. The
target is a prompt-injection detector, so the project measures the robustness of a
mitigation for OWASP LLM01: Prompt Injection.

## This site

The [blog](posts/index.qmd) documents the build as a series of computational
essays. The code, the full specification, and reproduction instructions are in the
[GitHub repository](https://github.com/turn1a/safety-classifier-transfer-risk).
